title: Top 10 LED Battens & Tubelights in India (2026)
description: Top 10 LED battens and tubelights in India for 2026: Syska, Wipro and Havells 4ft battens ranked on lumens, build and price for homes and sites.
author: Elume Editorial
intro: The 4ft LED batten is the workhorse of Indian lighting: it replaced the old 36-40W fluorescent tubelight with a sealed 20W fitting that needs no choke, no starter and no tube replacements, and it now lights everything from kitchens and shops to stairwells and site offices. Because battens are bought in quantity, small differences in price and build quality multiply across a project. This guide ranks the battens worth buying on Elume in 2026, judged on wattage, colour temperature, build and per-unit price, and then rounds out the list with the bulbs and outdoor fittings that belong on the same lighting order.

Shortlist criteria are simple for this category. Stick to 20W for a standard 4ft batten, expect 6500K cool daylight unless you specifically want warm light, check the body material (polycarbonate resists rust in kitchens and bathrooms), and look at input voltage range, since wide-range drivers such as 140-270V survive Indian voltage swings far better. Finally, compare the real Elume price against MRP: batten margins vary wildly between brands, and the discount often decides the ranking as much as the spec sheet does.

items.name: 20W LED Batten 4ft
items.body: Syska's 20W 4ft batten is the value benchmark of this list and our default recommendation for volume buying. It delivers standard 6500K cool daylight from a polycarbonate body that shrugs off damp kitchens, bathrooms and semi-open passages where metal fittings rust. At around Rs 355 on Elume against a Rs 550 MRP, it is cheap enough to light an entire flat or shop without the lighting line dominating the budget, and consistent enough that mixing units across rooms looks uniform.
items.bestFor: Best value batten for whole-home buys

items.name: Garnet 20W LED Batten 4ft
items.body: Wipro's Garnet matches the Syska on the basics, 20W, 4ft, 6500K cool daylight, and adds a slimmer profile that sits more discreetly against the ceiling or wall. Wipro's lighting pedigree is among the oldest in India, which counts for something in consistency of light colour between batches, a real issue when you buy battens over multiple orders. At around Rs 385 on Elume it costs a shade more than the Syska, a fair premium for the neater look.

items.name: Austra Arc 20W LED Batten
items.body: The Austra Arc is Havells' mainstream batten, and its headline advantage is the wide 140-270V AC input range. In areas with weak or swinging supply, cheap battens flicker, dim or die early; a wide-range driver rides through the fluctuation. You also get Havells' distribution and service reach, useful when a unit needs replacing under warranty in a smaller town. At around Rs 931 on Elume it is a clear step up in price, justified where power quality is poor.

items.name: Elenor Plus Broad 20W LED Batten
items.body: The Elenor Plus is Havells' broad-profile batten: the wider diffuser spreads light more softly and reduces the harsh linear glare a narrow batten throws in low-ceiling rooms. It shares the robust 140-270V input range with the Austra Arc, so it handles unstable supply equally well. At around Rs 1,225 on Elume it is the most expensive batten here, which limits it to main living spaces, offices and shops where light quality is worth paying for over raw lumens per rupee.
items.bestFor: Softer, broader light in living spaces

items.name: 9W LED Bulb B22 (pack of 2)
items.body: No batten order is complete without bulbs for the holders battens cannot reach: bathrooms, balconies, lofts and lamp fittings. Philips' 9W B22 pack of 2 delivers the same 6500K cool daylight as the battens above, so rooms lit by a mix of both stay colour-matched. Philips remains the reference for lumen maintenance, meaning the bulb stays bright rather than fading early. At around Rs 315 a pack on Elume, keep one pack per room as the standard ratio.

items.name: LED Bulb 9W (pack of 4)
items.body: For pure bulk economics, Syska's 9W four-pack undercuts everything: around Rs 540 on Elume works out to roughly Rs 135 a bulb. Same 6500K daylight tone, same B22 base, so they slot into any standard holder in the house. This is the pack to buy for rental properties, staff rooms, store rooms and stairwells, anywhere you need reliable light and the bulb is a consumable rather than a fitting you fuss over. Pair with Syska battens to keep one brand across the site.

items.name: 30W LED Flood Light
items.body: Once the indoor tubes are sorted, most homes and sites need at least one flood light for the gate, terrace or parking area. Syska's 30W flood carries an IP66 rating, sealed tightly enough to take direct monsoon rain, and throws 6500K daylight over a wide area. At around Rs 940 on Elume it is the affordable way to cover outdoor security lighting on the same order as your battens, sharing the same brand and colour temperature for a consistent install.

items.name: Centura Neo LED Flood Light 20W
items.body: The Centura Neo is the branded alternative for smaller outdoor areas: 20W of cool day white output with a 2-year warranty and zero mercury content. It suits compact facades, entrances and shop signboards where a 30W flood would be overkill. At around Rs 921 on Elume it costs nearly the same as Syska's larger flood, so choose it for the warranty and Havells build rather than raw output. One of these over the main door is a sensible default on any new wiring job.

items.name: 24W LED Street Light
items.body: For driveways, society lanes and farmhouse approaches, a proper street light beats an angled flood. Philips' 24W unit is IP65 rated with the driver on board, so it wires directly to a pole or wall arm with no separate box, and it emits the same 6500K daylight as the rest of this list. At around Rs 1,690 on Elume it is a serious fitting, but it is engineered to run dusk to dawn every night, which is exactly what cheap fittings fail at.

items.name: Flexion AC Rope Light IP65 50m (Warm White)
items.body: The decorative slot on this list goes to Havells' Flexion rope light: a 50m warm white reel rated IP65 for indoor and outdoor use at 8W per metre. It is the professional answer for cove lighting, balcony railings, terraces and festival decoration, and buying one long reel beats stringing together short consumer strips that fail at every joint. At around Rs 4,900 on Elume for the full 50m, cut and deploy it across the whole house from a single purchase.

buyingTips: A 20W LED batten replaces an old 36-40W fluorescent tubelight fitting, and it does so with no choke, starter or tube to replace later. If you are still running fluorescent fittings, the energy saving plus zero maintenance makes the swap an easy call.

Check the input voltage range if your area has unstable supply: wide-range drivers such as the 140-270V units on the Havells Austra Arc and Elenor Plus survive fluctuations that kill narrow-range battens early.

Keep one colour temperature per room. Mixing 6500K daylight battens with warm white bulbs in the same space looks patchy; either standardise on 6500K throughout or deliberately zone warm light to bedrooms and cove lighting.
Prefer polycarbonate bodies for kitchens, bathrooms and semi-open areas. They do not rust, and the Syska 20W batten's polycarbonate build is a big part of why it holds up in damp Indian conditions.

Order indoor battens, spare bulbs and outdoor floods together in one consolidated Elume order. It keeps every fitting on the same delivery and lets you standardise brand and colour temperature across the whole property in one decision.

faq.q: What is the difference between an LED batten and a tubelight?
faq.a: An LED batten is the modern replacement for the tubelight: instead of a separate tube, choke and starter, the LEDs, driver and diffuser come sealed in one slim fitting that mounts straight to the wall or ceiling. There is no tube to replace; when a batten eventually fails after years of use, you replace the fitting.

faq.q: Is 20W enough, or do I need a higher wattage batten?
faq.a: For a standard Indian room, one 20W 4ft batten gives roughly the light of the old 36-40W fluorescent tube it replaced and is enough as a primary light. Large halls or deep shops simply use two or more battens spaced evenly rather than one higher-wattage unit, which also spreads light more uniformly.

faq.q: What does 6500K mean and should I choose it?
faq.a: 6500K is cool daylight, the crisp white light most Indian homes and shops use for general lighting, and it is the standard for the battens and bulbs in this list. Warm white (around 2700-3000K) looks yellowish and cosier, better suited to bedrooms, lamps and decorative cove lighting than to task areas like kitchens.

faq.q: Why do some LED battens fail early in Indian homes?
faq.a: The usual culprits are voltage fluctuation and heat, not the LEDs themselves. Cheap narrow-range drivers degrade quickly where supply swings; battens with wide 140-270V input ranges, like the Havells models here, are built for exactly those conditions. Buying from established brands with real warranties is the practical protection.

faq.q: Can I use these battens in a bathroom or covered balcony?
faq.a: Standard battens suit bathrooms and covered balconies as long as they are not hit by direct water spray; a polycarbonate body like the Syska batten's also avoids rust in damp air. For fittings exposed to rain outdoors, use a properly rated fitting instead, such as an IP65 or IP66 flood or street light.
